question: Design a `Printer` class with an overloaded public method `print`. This method should be able to print: 1) an integer, 2) a double, and 3) a C-style string (`const char*`). Demonstrate printing different data types using the same method name.

test_cases.input: No direct input, uses hardcoded calls.
test_cases.expected:
--- Testing Readability / Efficiency Issue ---
Printing an integer: 12345
Printing a double: 98.765
Printing a C-style string: Another string example
Printing a C-style string: (null)
Printing a C-style string: Short
Printing a C-style string: A very long string that might highlight the overhead of unnecessary string conversion and allocation for C-style strings, especially if done repeatedly.
--- End Readability / Efficiency Issue Test ---

errors: All print methods use `std::endl` which flushes the output buffer on each call, potentially reducing efficiency. Using `\n` is generally preferred unless an immediate flush is explicitly required.

The `print(const char*)` method creates a temporary `std::string` from the C-style string, incurring an unnecessary memory allocation and copy operation. Direct printing using `std::cout << value` (with a `nullptr` check) would be more efficient.
